title: Denali faces battle against dandelions
summary: Denali faces battle against dandelions The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - Intruders are making a relentless advance and threatening to overrun Denali National Park and Preserve. Park experts warn of a possible "crisis" stretching as far as Wonder Lake. The invaders are dandelions. The fuzzy, yellow flowers might not seem like a major environmental scourge, but park officials say they must be taken seriously to prevent existing wildflowers and other native plant species from being choked out. To fight the battle, Denali vegetation technician Jean Balay is trying to line up an army of weed-eradicators to attack acres and acres of non-native dandelions springing up across Denali. "We've given up the fight," she said of the situation near the park entrance and main visitors center. "It's out of …

title: Kenai brown bear hunt canceled
summary: Kenai brown bear hunt canceled By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Too many female brown bears have died this year on the Kenai Peninsula to allow an annual autumn bear hunt, state biologists said Wednesday. The announcement brings the fall hunt's cancellation streak to four years in a row. Between successful hunting in the spring and shootings in defense of life and property this summer, the Peninsula already has reached its tiny quota of 14 dead brown bears, said Ted Spraker, area wildlife management biologist. No more than six of those 14 should be females, according to a formula designed to maintain a bear population of approximately 250 animals. "We've exceeded the number of females taken this year - we've taken seven - so our season is over," Spraker said. The Penins…

title: New rules, size for book panel
summary: New rules, size for book panel By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter The Anchorage School Board decided Monday to take more direct control of the committee that advises the district on book-banning decisions. The committee twice clashed with the board last year on treatment of the book "American Indian Myths and Legends," which contains some sexually explicit stories. Under the plan approved Monday, the board instead of the superintendent will appoint members of the Controversial Issues Review Committee, and the committee will be enlarged so that members from the public outnumber the combined total of students, teachers, librarians and school principals. In addition, the board plans to ask applicants to the committee to fill out a form that includes their philosophy on book banning. Th…

title: Assembly to buy volcano center site
summary: Assembly to buy volcano center site By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Dreams for a Kenai Peninsula-based volcano research center, dormant for a year, have rumbled back to life. The Peninsula Borough Assembly voted 5-4 Tuesday to spend $315,000 on 80 scenic acres south of Ninilchik where the $12.5 million North Pacific Volcano Learning Center is to be built. "It's a major, major milestone," said Emmitt Trimble, president of the center's board of directors. The board will pay an additional $85,000 to bring the final sale price to $400,000. Fund-raising for the center was on hold for about a year while the Assembly wrestled over buying the land. With the purchase, promoters again can seek grants from state and federal agencies, which won't commit to financial aid unless the…

title: Coast Guardsmen free Homer whale
summary: Coast Guardsmen free Homer whale By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Two U.S. Coast Guard crewmen thought twice before approaching the 20- to 25-foot humpback whale. Their inflatable skiff was only 15 feet long. But the whale was exhausted, like a played out salmon, lying on the surface of Homer's Mud Bay with its tail fin wrapped in nylon rope. A fishing buoy and what appeared to be a crab pot were in tow. A passing tug had notified the National Marine Fisheries Service shortly before 7 p.m. Thursday that the whale had been thrashing around for a couple of hours. The agency called the Coast Guard cutter Roanoke Island, a 110-foot patrol boat stationed at Homer's boat harbor, and asked it to take a look. So Gunner's mate Wally Burgess and Fireman John Barton launched a rig…

title: Knowles names panel to look at salmon woes
summary: Knowles names panel to look at salmon woes By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter A former gubernatorial candidate, a university dean and the mayor of a Yukon River village hard hit by two years of dismal salmon runs are among seven members of a task force appointed by Gov. Tony Knowles to lay the groundwork for a scientific investigation of the Bering Sea fishery disaster. Knowles charged the task force with reviewing what is known about how changes in climate, ocean conditions, pollution and other factors that may have influenced the drastic decline in salmon returns to Bristol Bay and the Bering Sea during the last two years. The group will make preparations for further study by a panel of scientists. "Before the science panel begins its work, we need to identify what science already knows a…

title: Inlet's seafood scores A+ in EPA toxins test
summary: Inlet's seafood scores A+ in EPA toxins test By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Cook Inlet's salmon, halibut and clams are as pristine as the Environmental Protection Agency has ever tested. "The seafood samples in Cook Inlet show some of the lowest levels of contamination that we've ever seen in sampling done throughout the country," said Rick Albright, director of the EPA's Alaska office. "These are some of the cleanest fish we've seen." A variety of fish and plants were gathered last summer near four of the Inlet's Native villages: Seldovia, Tyonek, Port Graham and Nanwalek. The EPA collected samples alongside villagers so that the food sent to labs was representative of what shows up on the dinner table, Albright said. The EPA completed the study after village residen…

title: Young bristles over Tongass roads
summary: Young bristles over Tongass roads By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - The House on Thursday refused to back off its plan to allow more logging roads in Alaska's two national forests following a debate Wednesday night that touched off a tirade by Rep. Don Young. Young ended up apologizing to Rep. Maurice Hinchey, saying his words were aimed at the upstate New York Democrat's amendment - which he characterized as "corrupt" and "immoral" - not the congressman himself. Hinchey had proposed an amendment to remove a 30-mile road easement though the Chugach National Forest where Natives want to log 8,000 acres in the Copper River Delta. The Hinchey amendment was rejected 249-176. The House also turned back, by a 237-186 vote, an amendment by California Rep. George Miller, t…

title: Homesteading gains renewed push
summary: Homesteading gains renewed push By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau Location of Point Possession. SOLDOTNA - Alaska homesteading died in 1986, but a Kenai Peninsula Borough Assembly member wants to revive it on a local level. Assembly President Jack Brown of Nikiski envisions a scaled-down version of the legendary federal program that deeded land to hardy souls who settled the wilderness. The modern equivalent would not be a land give-away, Brown said. But participants would get price discounts of up to 80 percent for building a home and living on their property, he said. He's looking at 8,129 acres of borough-owned land at Point Possession, at the northernmost tip of the Kenai Peninsula. "It would be one of the last large places to homestead on the Peninsula," Brown said. "It's a ch…

title: Breast cancer fight wins praise
summary: Breast cancer fight wins praise Women awarded for traveling clinic By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - The idea started with a group of Fairbanks women talking about the plight of a friend who had to fly to Seattle for a mammogram because there was no equipment available in town. Nearly 25 years later, two of those women - Nancy Murkowski and Mary Jane Fate - were honored at a luncheon Thursday in a marble hall of the Senate for creating the Breast Cancer Detection Center, which has grown into a traveling clinic reaching women in more than 80 remote villages. By land, air and barge, the Fairbanks-based center is taking equipment into villages to fight breast cancer, which will strike an estimated 200 women this year in Alaska and kill 50 of them. "We decided that the…

title: Election funding rule appealed
summary: Election funding rule appealed By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter The Alaska campaign finance reform law, declared unconstitutional last month by a Superior Court judge, provides an essential element of our form of democracy: Public trust that the government is of the people and for the people, Assistant Attorney General David Jones told the Alaska Supreme Court on Wednesday. The 1996 law assures citizens that their voices won't be drowned out by heavily monied corporations and Outside interests, Jones said in appealing the Superior Court decision throwing out the law. But an attorney for the Alaska Civil Liberties Union argued that the law is an impermissible attempt by the government to tell people how much free speech is enough. "Less money can only mean less speech, less money to commun…

title: Mystery remains identified
summary: Mystery remains identified Troopers say skin bits, suit were fisherman's Working with a torn, battered survival suit and a few bits of human skin, Alaska State Troopers have identified the remains of a fisherman who died seven months earlier and 350 miles away. David Paul Hanlon, 47, of Juneau was lost at sea about 80 miles southeast of Yakutat when the fishing boat LaConte sank Jan. 31. Coast Guard helicopter pilots and rescue swimmers braved 40-foot seas to pluck three other crewmembers from the water alive. Mark Morley, the LaConte's skipper, died. His body was recovered. Hanlon's fate remained a mystery until Monday, when a technician at the state crime lab in Anchorage matched a fingerprint taken from remains found in the survival suit to Hanlon's prints, which troopers had on file. I…

title: Lawsuit aims to boot Lindauer from ballot
summary: Lawsuit aims to boot Lindauer from ballot By SHEILA TOOMEY and STEVE RINEHART Daily News reporters Complaints that gubernatorial candidate John Lindauer failed to tell voters enough about where he got his money moved into Superior Court on Wednesday in a lawsuit filed by a Chugiak woman who wants to stop Lindauer from being certified as the official winner of the Republican primary election. Frances E. Grady, identified only as "a qualified Alaska voter," filed suit against the state Division of Elections, asking that Lindauer's name be kept off the Nov. 3 ballot or that he be barred from taking office if he runs and wins. Lindauer's campaign manager, Tuckerman Babcock, dismissed the suit as a rehash of old allegations, "just an effort to try to generate some continuing publicity." The sui…

title: Knowles offers disaster aid funds
summary: Knowles offers disaster aid funds Lawmakers line up behind $19 million for Western Alaska By HELEN JUNG Daily News reporter Gov. Tony Knowles on Thursday declared Western Alaska communities an economic disaster area and proposed a $19 million package of state and federal funds to help residents cope with this summer's meager salmon returns. Knowles asked legislative leaders to authorize $12 million for needs including food, fuel and electricity for the 8,000 families affected in the fishery-dependent Bristol Bay, Yukon River and Kuskokwim River regions. This is the second year in a row that salmon returns have been at such poor levels that the governor has declared economic disasters in those regions. This summer, the runs are the worst in two decades, and families are worrying they won't …

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Gravel train derails No one hurt, but rides to Denali will have to wait By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Tourists, fuel and gravel won't be traveling by train through parts of the state, at least until tonight, after an Alaska Railroad train derailed at Elmendorf Air Force Base early Thursday. No one was injured when the gravel-hauling train jumped the tracks just after midnight. But with 10 cars smashed together like an accordion and six others tipped on their side, Alaska Railroad officials said the accident would disrupt passenger and freight traffic between Anchorage and Fairbanks for at least 48 hours. The train lurched off the tracks in roughly the middle of its 79 cars and spilled some 600 tons of gravel. The first six cars tipped on their side and tore up track and ties. On…

title: Lindauer has GOP nod - for now
summary: Lindauer has GOP nod - for now Party's executive committee plans another meeting By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter A beaming John Lindauer emerged Monday from a 90-minute closed-door meeting with the executive committee of the Alaska Republican Committee with his party endorsement intact, at least for the time being. Lindauer described his discussions with the party's executive committee as mainly focusing on campaign strategies, "especially how to get all the candidates elected." He said he didn't think the question of the party withdrawing its support for him ever came up. So far, Lindauer's interpretation of the closed meeting is the only one available. Most of the committee left without commenting. Party Chairman Tom McKay said Lindauer "did some very positive and constructive things" …

title: Wolf snare ban back on November's ballot
summary: Wolf snare ban back on November's ballot By STEVE RINEHART Daily News reporter A question asking voters if they want to ban wolf snaring will be placed on the November election ballot, the Alaska Supreme Court ruled on Monday. The high court overturned a Superior Court decision in May that a citizen petition to ban wolf snares was unconstitutional. Fairbanks Judge Ralph Beistline said the state constitution gives the Legislature the authority to manage the state's wildlife and that it would be "inappropriate to dictate the method or tool." The Supreme Court's brief order Monday did not address the arguments for or against the petition. It simply reversed Beistline and directed that "the initiative shall be placed on the ballot." The ruling drew praise from the winners and dismay from the l…

title: English initiative foes gear for fight
summary: English initiative foes gear for fight By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Calling the measure divisive and hurtful, a group has formed to fight an initiative that would make English Alaska's official language. "We don't see this as a benign, inoffensive bill," said April Ferguson, general counsel for Bristol Bay Native Corp. and one of the measure's critics. "This is something with real chilling effects." The group, Alaskans Against English Only Ballot Number 6, registered with the state as a political action committee in mid-August and held its first organizational meeting last week at the Native corporation's office. Those attending the meeting said they oppose the ballot measure because it might prevent Alaska Natives from conducting meetings in Native languages and harm bilingual…

title: Village Charter School folds
summary: Village Charter School folds By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter A charter school that opened three weeks ago in the Campbell Creek Science Center abruptly shut down over the weekend, forcing families to keep their children at home or quickly enroll them elsewhere. Teachers and organizers of the Village Charter School, which served 27 to 29 students in kindergarten through eighth grade, said the classroom space at the science center wasn't a good home for the school because of cramped quarters and too many interruptions. Assistant Superintendent Carol Comeau said Monday she was surprised at the sudden decision and is concerned about the children being out of school. Superintendent Bob Christal announced at the beginning of Monday night's School Board meeting that the school will be o…

title: Crack in finance law helped incumbents
summary: Crack in finance law helped incumbents By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter Critics of Alaska's campaign finance reform have argued that the law helps incumbents get re-elected and hurts challengers. "I call it the Incumbent Protection Act of 1996," said Jon Rubini, an attorney challenging the legality of the law on behalf of the Alaska Civil Liberties Union. By limiting the supply of campaign money, he and others reason, the law stacks the deck in favor of those already in office because they are the ones who can draw the dollars even when the rules are restrictive. So what happened when a Superior Court judge lifted the strict rules for five days in August? Money flooded into the campaigns of incumbents while pocket change rolled toward challengers, according to the mandatory reports contri…

title: More reds find river; run's still weak
summary: More reds find river; run's still weak By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - With 40,000 red salmon in the Kenai River Tuesday and more pushing upstream Wednesday, biologists held off on a complete red salmon fishing closure. Commercial fishermen, meanwhile, have chalked up the season as a loss and have started to ask if the weak red run qualifies as a regional economic disaster. The river on Wednesday still was roughly 100,000 salmon short of the state's minimum sonar count of 550,000 reds, designed to guarantee adequate spawning for future runs. "It's hour by hour, but we do have a little bit of breathing room," said Dave Nelson, area sportfisheries management biologist. More reds were swimming past the sonar counter Wednesday afternoon, biologists said. Nelson cautioned t…

title: Salmon sharks find Sound to their taste
summary: Salmon sharks find Sound to their taste Scientists at loss to explain jump in numbers By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter When a group of scientists conducting forage fish surveys in Prince William Sound's Galena Bay got an odd reading on their underwater acoustic equipment, they lowered an underwater camera into the sea. What they saw were salmon sharks. Hundreds of them. Maybe 1,000. Where did they come from? "I fished in the Sound for years and didn't see wiggle one until I was doing research out there in 1995," said Lee Hulbert, research fisheries biologist for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in Juneau. "They were everywhere." Luke Boer, who operates Native Sun Charters and Tours in Cordova and has fished the Sound for more than 30 years, reports their numbers o…

title: At Hyder, only the good behavior of the bears makes creek safe
summary: At Hyder, only the good behavior of the bears makes creek safe By MIKE DOOGAN One of the places I went while I was off was Hyder. One of the things I did there was watch bears fish. I also watched people watch bears fish. The fish were plentiful and the bears well-behaved. Good thing, because the people were almost as plentiful and not so well-behaved. Hyder is a cluster of funky buildings at the end of the road. The town is in Alaska, but just barely. The road to it runs through British Columbia, and a couple of miles away is the town of Stewart, B.C. Hyder claims 100 residents, but I think some people must have snuck back into the line while they were counting. Stewart, which claims 1,000 residents, contains all the necessities of civilization: the school, the hospital, the bank, the goo…

title: Wolf control 'irrelevant,' critic says
summary: Wolf control 'irrelevant,' critic says The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - A longtime critic of state wolf control measures says the Alaska Department of Fish and Game's plan to boost caribou in the Fortymile herd is not working because caribou are not migrating as expected. Gordon Haber of Denali Park contends that most Fortymile caribou calving this spring occurred outside the agency's control zone where wolves have been sterilized or removed to reduce predation. After calving, the herd's migration skirted the boundary of the control area and only dipped into a portion of its northeast corner, he said. In a letter sent last week to Fish and Game commissioner Frank Rue, Haber said the sterilization and relocation efforts "have turned out to be almost irrelevant so far. They have served only …

title: New speaker system installed in Atwood Hall sounds like winner
summary: New speaker system installed in Atwood Hall sounds like winner By MIKE DUNHAM Daily News arts editor When Gladys Knight sings "Midnight Train to Georgia" in Atwood Hall tonight, fans won't just hear the soul diva's voice. They'll also be listening to a new $125,000 state-of-the-art sound system that, according to engineers, solves some of the acoustical problems that have plagued the Alaska Center for the Performing Arts since it opened a decade ago. The new system promises to deliver Knight's songs with more accuracy, crisper articulation, less distortion and an absence of errant noise, said the center's technical director, Dale Chavie. What's more, people in the remote corners of the hall will get the same sound as listeners sitting front and center. In addition to better sound for patro…

title: National ad spurs outcry over wolf hunting, trapping in Denali
summary: National ad spurs outcry over wolf hunting, trapping in Denali The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - An animal rights group's advertisement in a national newspaper has prompted more than 70 calls to Denali National Park and Preserve to protest the hunting and trapping of wolves. The ad by Friends of Animals appeared Monday in USA Today. It shows people in a park shuttle bus watching an adult wolf and three pups cross the Denali Park road. The headline reads, "The Toklat wolves learn to trust park visitors in June and July. This makes it easier for hunters to shoot them in August." The ad says the Toklat pack is threatened by "virtually unrestricted" hunting and trapping in about half of Denali from Aug. 10 to April 30. The state also allows hunting and trapping just outside the park, the ad say…

title: Knowles given bipartisan backing
summary: Knowles given bipartisan backing By STEVE RINEHART Daily News reporter Democratic Gov. Tony Knowles got a campaign boost from Republicans as well as from people in large and small businesses around the state on Wednesday. More than 100 of them, including former legislators and industry captains, agreed to join a public list of Knowles' supporters. The governor, battling a well-heeled Republican challenger and a selection of minor-party candidates, proclaimed that the list shows he has backing from people across party, social and economic lines in Alaska. "Alaska's economy is healthy," he said in a press conference at his campaign office in downtown Anchorage, flanked by some of the business people on the list, "and these Alaskans want it to continue in that direction." Knowles ticked off w…

title: Solvent dumping claimed
summary: Solvent dumping claimed Princess Cruises disputes fired worker's charge By JIM CLARKE The Associated Press An environmental group and a former employee on Wednesday accused Princess Cruises of dumping oil, grease and solvents into a waste pit and a marshy area adjacent to its former bus maintenance garage. Douglas Bachman worked for the tourism company for a year, but was fired in April 1997 after vapors from degreasers in the shop sickened him. He accuses Princess of firing him because he told government regulators that the company was dumping the chemicals onto the ground. At a news conference Wednesday in Anchorage, Bachman and Alaska Community Action on Toxics called for an investigation and cleanup, and released test results they say show significant levels of chlorinated solvents in …

title: Taking control of growth
summary: Taking control of growth New group sees need for planning in Mat-Su By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau PALMER - A new exhibit at this year's state fair is tucked between giant zucchini and bottles of pickled produce. Made with Legos, model planes and miniature cars, it portrays an ideal community - one where businesses and homes are separated and agricultural land protected. Labeled "Planning for Gold," the display is the public debut of Friends of Mat-Su. The new group is made up of Valley residents frustrated by what they see as a lack of land-use planning in Mat-Su. It's modeled after the Portland-based 1,000 Friends of Oregon, a powerful citizens group that advocates for higher density development combined with the preservation of open space. Although organizers say they don'…

title: Mother gets probation for child assault
summary: Mother gets probation for child assault Anchorage woman freed, ordered to get counseling By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter Kathleen Tozer spent five months in jail awaiting trial for pressing a pillow on her 10-year-old daughter's face last year. That's enough time, a state Superior Court judge said Wednesday. In addition to time served, Judge Larry Card sentenced Tozer, 38, to three years of probation for second-degree assault and ordered her to get extensive psychological help. What she did was wrong, despite her long-simmering frustration with the emotionally disturbed child, Card said. "But for a moment of some sanity when she listened to her other daughter, we might be dealing with a murder case," he said. "A pillow, though not usually thought of as a deadly instrument, can be just …

title: Gingrich wraps up visit, urges caution in ANWR development decision
summary: Gingrich wraps up visit, urges caution in ANWR development decision By JIM CLARKE The Associated Press After getting a long weekend full of Alaska, U.S. House Speaker Newt Gingrich appears to have reached the conclusion that his political hosts hoped he would reach: To understand Alaska, you have to see it. Gingrich said Monday his four-day visit to the state gave him a fuller appreciation for the vast distances the state covers and a better understanding of the oil industry. That's not to say he embraces everything the state's congressional delegation urges about North Slope oil development. Wrapping up his visit with a series of news conferences, Gingrich, R-Ga., reiterated his cautious approach toward opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge's coastal plain for development. Gingrich …

title: Babbitt NPR-A plan meets early criticism
summary: Babbitt NPR-A plan meets early criticism By T.A. BADGER Daily News reporter As expected, Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt on Thursday approved opening the National Petroleum Reserve-Alaska to oil and gas leasing, and just as expected, both industry and environmentalists are less than elated with the way he's doing it. Oil companies are unhappy that Babbitt's plan for the North Slope reserve won't allow them to look for oil where it's most likely to be found. And conservationists are upset that valuable habitat may not be protected as well as it should be. Babbitt's so-called "preferred alternative" for opening 4 million acres in the reserve's northeast corner creates a land-use patchwork within the tundra, lakes and coastal wetlands, which is believed to sit atop large quantities of crude.…

title: Scientists warm up to UAF's new climate-research facility
summary: Scientists warm up to UAF's new climate-research facility Arctic Research Center to focus on world's warming trend By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter FAIRBANKS - Syun-Ichi Akasofu remembers the year he arrived at the University of Alaska Fairbanks as a time when the Geophysical Institute was buzzing with brainpower. It was 1958, the International Geophysical Year, and scientists from all over the world were sharing research. Data on volcanoes, sea ice, the aurora borealis and other natural phenomena poured into tiny Fairbanks. International scientists, such as young Akasofu from Japan, followed. "You could hear Italian-English, Pakistani-English, all kinds of languages in the halls," recalled Akasofu, an expert on the northern lights who now directs UAF's Geophysical Institute. "It w…

title: Kids' tire fire became fatal blaze in minutes
summary: Kids' tire fire became fatal blaze in minutes By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter Four-year-old Wayne Benson, his brother and their cousin squeezed through a 6-inch gap to get into the crawl space under the play tower in the churchyard. They set a tiny fire inside a tire they found inside. The oldest boy, 9-year-old Bobby LeMoine, was outside gathering more sticks when the tire caught fire, emitting intense heat and smoke. Six-year-old Justin Benson escaped. Wayne was trapped inside. He died of smoke inhalation. His mother was among those standing helplessly in the playground as firefighters fought the blaze. "It seemed like I waited a lifetime," Kim Malsbury said Monday, the day after her child died. "It's hard to let go," Malsbury said. "He was my baby." Fire Department officials said th…
